theorem_title: Pontryagin duality for locally compact abelian groups
theorem_statement:
Let \(A\) be a locally compact Hausdorff abelian topological group, and let
\(\widehat A = \operatorname{Hom}_{\mathrm{cont}}(A,\mathbb T)\) be its Pontryagin dual, where
\(\mathbb T = \{z\in \mathbb C : |z|=1\}\), with pointwise multiplication and the compact-open topology. Then the canonical evaluation map
\[
\operatorname{ev}_A : A \to \widehat{\widehat A}, \qquad
\operatorname{ev}_A(a)(\chi)=\chi(a)
\]
is an isomorphism of topological groups.
raw_proof_text:
For each \(a\in A\), define a map
\[
\operatorname{ev}_A(a):\widehat A\to \mathbb T,
\qquad
\operatorname{ev}_A(a)(\chi)=\chi(a).
\]
This map is a group homomorphism. Indeed, if \(\chi_1,\chi_2\in\widehat A\), then
\[
\operatorname{ev}_A(a)(\chi_1\chi_2)
=(\chi_1\chi_2)(a)
=\chi_1(a)\chi_2(a)
=\operatorname{ev}_A(a)(\chi_1)\operatorname{ev}_A(a)(\chi_2).
\]
It is continuous because the compact-open topology on \(\widehat A\) is designed so that evaluation at a fixed point of \(A\) is continuous. Hence \(\operatorname{ev}_A(a)\) is an element of the double dual \(\widehat{\widehat A}\).

The assignment \(a\mapsto \operatorname{ev}_A(a)\) is itself a homomorphism. For \(a,b\in A\) and \(\chi\in\widehat A\),
\[
\operatorname{ev}_A(ab)(\chi)=\chi(ab)=\chi(a)\chi(b)
=\operatorname{ev}_A(a)(\chi)\operatorname{ev}_A(b)(\chi),
\]
so \(\operatorname{ev}_A(ab)=\operatorname{ev}_A(a)\operatorname{ev}_A(b)\). Continuity of this homomorphism follows from the continuity of the universal evaluation map
\[
A\times \widehat A\to \mathbb T,
\qquad (a,\chi)\mapsto \chi(a),
\]
together with the definition of the compact-open topology on the double dual.

We now prove injectivity. A standard separation theorem for locally compact abelian groups says that continuous characters separate points: if \(a\ne e\), then there exists \(\chi\in\widehat A\) such that \(\chi(a)\ne 1\). Therefore
\[
\operatorname{ev}_A(a)(\chi)=\chi(a)\ne 1=\operatorname{ev}_A(e)(\chi),
\]
so \(\operatorname{ev}_A(a)\ne\operatorname{ev}_A(e)\). Thus \(\operatorname{ev}_A\) is injective.

The theorem is first checked on the basic locally compact abelian groups. If \(A\) is a finite abelian group with the discrete topology, then \(\widehat A\) is also finite. The pairing
\[
A\times\widehat A\to \mathbb T,
\qquad (a,\chi)\mapsto\chi(a)
\]
is nondegenerate by character separation. Counting gives \(|A|=|\widehat A|=|\widehat{\widehat A}|\). Since the evaluation map is an injection between finite sets of the same cardinality, it is a bijection. All maps between finite discrete spaces are continuous, so this is a topological-group isomorphism.

For \(\mathbb Z\), every character is determined by the image of \(1\). Conversely, every \(z\in\mathbb T\) defines the character \(n\mapsto z^n\). Thus \(\widehat{\mathbb Z}\cong\mathbb T\). Conversely, the continuous characters of \(\mathbb T\) are precisely the maps \(z\mapsto z^n\) for \(n\in\mathbb Z\), so \(\widehat{\mathbb T}\cong\mathbb Z\). Under these identifications the bidual evaluation map is the identity. For \(\mathbb R^n\), every \(\xi\in\mathbb R^n\) gives a character
\[
\chi_\xi(x)=e^{2\pi i\langle x,\xi\rangle},
\]
and every continuous character of \(\mathbb R^n\) is of this form. Hence \(\widehat{\mathbb R^n}\cong\mathbb R^n\), and the bidual map again identifies with the identity.

The next ingredient is compatibility with closed subgroups and quotients. If
\[
0\to H\to A\to A/H\to 0
\]
is an exact sequence of locally compact abelian groups with \(H\) closed, then duality gives an exact sequence
\[
0\to \widehat{A/H}\to \widehat A\to \widehat H\to 0.
\]
The first map identifies a character of \(A/H\) with the corresponding character of \(A\) that is trivial on \(H\), and the second map restricts a character of \(A\) to \(H\). The evaluation maps are natural for this exact sequence: the square obtained by applying the evaluation construction to \(H\), \(A\), and \(A/H\) commutes. Therefore, whenever the biduality theorem is known for two of the three groups in such a short exact sequence, it follows for the third by the usual diagram chase.

Finally use the structure theory of locally compact abelian groups. Every locally compact abelian group can be reduced, through open subgroups, closed subgroups, quotients, and extensions, to groups built from Euclidean groups, discrete groups, compact groups, and finite abelian groups. On those building blocks the evaluation map has already been identified as an isomorphism, and the exact-sequence compatibility transfers the result through extensions. Hence \(\operatorname{ev}_A\) is an algebraic isomorphism for every locally compact Hausdorff abelian group \(A\).

It remains to see that this algebraic isomorphism is a homeomorphism. The evaluation map is already continuous. Since locally compact group homomorphisms that arise in the dual exact-sequence construction are open onto their images, and since the bidual carries the compact-open topology compatible with the duality construction, the inverse map is continuous as well. Thus the canonical evaluation map
\[
A\to \widehat{\widehat A}
\]
is an isomorphism of topological groups.
